citation: Pollock JR, Doan MK, Asprey WL, Dulle DL, Moore ML, Makovicka JL, Hassebrock JD, Foley BM, Chhabra A. Can NFL Combine Results be Used to Estimate NFL Defensive Players Longevity? Sports Medicine International Open. 2021;5:E59-E64.
abstract: This retrospective study examined the relationship between NFL Scouting Combine drill performance and career longevity for all defensive players who competed in the combine from 2005 to 2015. Combine results (height, weight, BMI, 10-/20-/40-yard dash, bench press, vertical jump, broad jump, short shuttle, 3-cone drill) were collected from NFLdraftscout.com and normalized by position using ratio-scaled BMI adjustments. Career length data (1-year and 5-year roster status) were cross-referenced from Pro Football Reference and NFL.com. Student's t-tests evaluated the influence of each performance metric on roster status, with significance set at p<0.05. The study found position-specific combine metrics that were significantly associated with both 1-year and 5-year career longevity.
tldr: Specific NFL Combine drill results — particularly the 20-yard shuttle, 40-yard dash, and 3-cone drill — are significantly associated with 1-year and 5-year career longevity for defensive players, with associations varying by position. NFL teams and medical staff can use these metrics as supplementary tools when assessing long-term career prospects for defensive players.
methods: Retrospective review of NFL Combine data (2005–2015) from NFLdraftscout.com for 339 cornerbacks, 356 linebackers, 185 safeties, and 278 inside defensive linemen. Combine results were ratio-scaled normalized by position using BMI. Career length (1-year and 5-year roster status) collected from ProFootballReference.com. Statistical analysis via Student's t-test in R 3.4.2; p<0.05 considered significant. No correction for multiple testing was applied.
findings:
• Linebackers: weight, 10-/20-/40-yard dash, broad jump, 3-cone drill, and shuttle all significantly associated with 1-year roster status; 10-/20-/40-yard dash, 3-cone drill, and shuttle associated with 5-year status.
• Cornerbacks: 20-/40-yard dash and broad jump significant for 1-year; weight, 10-/20-/40-yard dash, 3-cone drill, and shuttle significant for 5-year status.
• Inside defensive linemen: weight and 10-yard dash significant for 1-year; 40-yard dash and shuttle significant for 5-year status.
• Safeties: fewest significant associations — no drill linked to 1-year status; height and shuttle linked to 5-year status.
• 20-yard shuttle had the most significant associations across positions for both 1-year and 5-year roster status.
limitations: Reliance on publicly available combine data introduces potential recording inaccuracies. Normalized data used BMI-based ratio scaling rather than allometric adjustments, which may be less precise in athletes at body composition extremes. No correction for multiple comparisons. Injury mechanism and player position depth were not accounted for. Small safety sample may have underpowered position-specific analyses.
practicalImplications: Combine drill performance — especially the 20-yard shuttle and 40-yard dash — can supplement scouting decisions for defensive positions. Staff should interpret results with caution at the individual level, as exceptional combine results do not guarantee longevity. Using normalized position-specific benchmarks may improve predictive accuracy over raw scores.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to NFL football defensive player evaluation. Position-specific combine benchmarks (LB: weight and multi-directional speed; CB: speed and agility; IDL: weight and short-area quickness) can guide athlete development programming to target traits most associated with career durability. Speed and agility training that improves 20-yard shuttle and 40-yard dash times may support longer career trajectories for defensive players.
rtp: Not a return-to-play study. However, understanding which physical traits predict career longevity may inform RTP decisions by highlighting baseline performance profiles that correlate with sustained NFL participation. Players returning from injury whose combine metrics fall into longevity-associated ranges may have better career outlooks.

citation: Sisk M, Medawar N, McClure M, Cooke B, Cannon R, Kufner D, D'Almeida S, Jardaly A, Asif I, Momaya A, Ponce B. Cardiovascular Disease in Retired NFL Players: A Systematic Review. The Physician and Sportsmedicine. 2024. DOI: 10.1080/00913847.2024.2315929.
abstract: This systematic review synthesized available literature on cardiovascular health outcomes in former NFL players using PRISMA guidelines and searches of PubMed, Scopus, and Embase. Sixteen studies met inclusion criteria, covering weight gain/obesity, mortality, obstructive sleep apnea, and cardiovascular disease risk factors. Though evidence was discordant across studies, former NFL players appeared to possess more favorable metabolic profiles and decreased mortality compared to community controls. Approximately 90% of former players were overweight or obese, and cardiovascular/cardiometabolic disease was identified as the leading cause of death in this population. Further research is necessary to establish the full impact of NFL play on cardiovascular health.
tldr: Despite high rates of overweight and obesity (90%), retired NFL players show comparable or more favorable cardiovascular and metabolic profiles than community controls, though cardiovascular disease remains their leading cause of death. Tailored preventive cardiovascular care strategies are needed for former players, particularly former linemen who show significantly higher rates of metabolic syndrome.
methods: Systematic review following PRISMA 27-item checklist. Searches conducted in PubMed, Embase, and Scopus (completed September 2021). Inclusion criteria: retired NFL players, cardiovascular-related outcomes, English-language, full-length publications. Exclusion: active players, case reports/series, commentaries, non-English studies. Risk of bias assessed independently by two reviewers using the Newcastle-Ottawa Quality Assessment Scale. Studies organized into four categories: weight gain/obesity, mortality, obstructive sleep apnea, and cardiovascular disease. Sixteen studies (n=122 to 3506 participants) met final inclusion criteria.
findings:
• 90% of former NFL players had overweight-range BMI (>24.9 kg/m²).
• Cardiovascular/cardiometabolic disease was the leading cause of death in both NFL-exposed cohorts and controls.
• Former linemen had 59.8% prevalence of metabolic syndrome vs. 30% for former non-linemen and 24% for US adult males.
• Compared to community controls matched for age, ethnicity, and BMI, former players had significantly lower prevalence of smoking, diabetes (p<0.01), sedentary lifestyle (p<0.05), and metabolic syndrome (p<0.05), but higher impaired fasting glucose (p<0.01).
• Former NFL players had a significantly higher risk of sleep apnea (27% vs. 11.5% of controls; p=0.002).
• BMI was associated with a 31% increase in coronary artery calcium prevalence (OR 1.31, 95% CI 1.11–1.56).
• Overall mortality was lower in former NFL players compared to US population controls.
• Weight gain from high school to college was significantly associated with cardiovascular disease (OR 1.08), chronic pain (OR 1.09), and sleep apnea (OR 1.13).
limitations: Evidence was discordant across studies due to heterogeneous study designs, control group selection, and data sources. Eleven of 16 studies used the same NFL Player Care Foundation/Living Heart Foundation Screening Program dataset, raising concern for overlap. Most studies are cross-sectional. BMI may be an inadequate measure for this population due to high muscle mass; percent body fat may be more appropriate. Selection bias may affect self-reported survey studies.
practicalImplications: Former linemen are a particularly high-risk group for metabolic syndrome and warrant proactive cardiovascular screening and lifestyle intervention programs post-retirement. Weight management strategies during playing career may reduce long-term cardiovascular risk. Obstructive sleep apnea screening should be standard for retired players, especially linemen. Despite general cardiovascular risk, former players show protective factors (less smoking, more physical activity history) that can be leveraged in preventive care.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to NFL football. Progressive weight gain from high school to professional play is linked to cardiovascular disease, sleep apnea, and chronic pain in later life. Monitoring body composition trajectories from recruiting through career transition and emphasizing healthy weight management as part of long-term athlete development may reduce post-career cardiometabolic risk, particularly for linemen.
rtp: Not a return-to-play study. However, cardiovascular screening findings may be relevant during RTP evaluations after cardiac events in current players. The high prevalence of metabolic syndrome risk factors in linemen, even during their active careers, suggests cardiovascular assessment should be integrated into pre-participation and return-to-play protocols.

citation: Grashow R, Tan CO, Thomas JD, DiGregorio H, Lee H, Rotem RS, Marengi D, Terry DP, Eagle SR, Iverson GL, Whittington AJ, Zafonte RD, Weisskopf MG, Baggish AL. Career Duration and Later-Life Health Conditions Among Former Professional American-Style Football Players. Occupational and Environmental Medicine. 2024;0:1-9.
abstract: This survey-based cross-sectional cohort study of 4,189 former professional American-style football (ASF) players examined associations between self-reported career duration and four self-reported health conditions: chronic pain, arthritis, mental health problems, and subjective cognitive symptoms. Using flexible regression with penalized B-splines to model non-linear relationships, the study found inverted U-shaped associations between career duration and all four health outcomes (all p<0.001), indicating adverse health effects were most common among men with intermediate career durations. Similar non-linear patterns were observed for four play-related exposures including concussion signs and symptoms, performance-enhancing drug use, intracareer surgeries, and average snaps per game. Findings challenge the assumption that career duration is a reliable linear proxy for neurotrauma exposure.
tldr: Former professional football players with intermediate career lengths (not the shortest or longest) showed the highest risk of chronic pain, arthritis, mental health problems, and cognitive symptoms, revealing an inverted U-shaped relationship that challenges the assumption that longer careers mean worse health outcomes. Researchers and clinicians should avoid using career duration as a simple linear proxy for cumulative health risk in former football players.
methods: Survey-based cross-sectional cohort study from the Football Players Health Study (FPHS) at Harvard University. Former professional ASF players (n=4,189; mean age 52±14 years; 39% Black, 34% lineman) recruited via residential and electronic mail. Health outcomes (chronic pain, arthritis, mental health problems, subjective cognitive symptoms via Neuro-QoL) self-reported. Career exposures included concussion signs and symptoms (CSS) score, performance-enhancing drug use, intracareer surgeries, and average snaps per game. Flexible regression with penalized B-splines and piecewise linear regression used to characterize non-linear career duration-outcome relationships. Models adjusted for age, race, and lineman status.
findings:
• Inverted U-shaped relationships observed between career duration and chronic pain, arthritis, mental health problems, and subjective cognitive symptoms (all p<0.001).
• Players with 4–8 seasons had significantly higher odds of chronic pain (OR 1.21; 95% CI 1.02–1.42; p=0.02) and arthritis compared to those with 0–3 seasons.
• Players with 13+ seasons had similar or lower odds of adverse health outcomes compared to the 0–3 season reference group.
• Piecewise regression identified breakpoints at 6.9 seasons (chronic pain), 9.5 (arthritis), 4.5 (mental health), and 7.0 (cognitive symptoms) where slope reversed.
• Similar inverted-U patterns observed for all four football-specific exposures (CSS score, snaps per game, performance-enhancing drugs, intracareer surgeries).
• Findings were consistent across lineman and non-lineman subgroups.
limitations: All outcomes and exposures were self-reported, lacking clinical confirmation. Recall bias possible, though findings were consistent with intracareer surgery data (salient events less subject to recall bias). Healthy worker survivor bias (HWSB) may explain attenuation in longer-career players: those remaining active longest may be biologically or psychologically more resilient. Snap data available for only a subset (n=1,980). Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference.
practicalImplications: Career duration is a poor and potentially misleading proxy for cumulative health risk in former ASF players. Clinicians and researchers should account for non-linear associations and consider using more direct exposure metrics (e.g., cumulative head impact index, total snaps). Medical providers caring for former players should not assume those with the longest careers are necessarily at greatest health risk — mid-career range players may carry the highest burden. Screening and intervention programs should target all career-length groups.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to NFL and professional American football. The finding that mid-range career players (roughly 5–12 seasons) show the highest health burden suggests that player development and athlete longevity programs should focus on reducing cumulative physical and neurological exposure during the middle portion of careers, not only at career endpoints. Monitoring concussion symptom burden and surgical load as ongoing exposure metrics may be more informative than career length alone.
rtp: Not a RTP study. However, the non-linear relationship between career duration and health outcomes — driven partly by concussion sign/symptom accumulation and intracareer surgeries — underscores that cumulative injury burden across a career significantly shapes long-term health. RTP decisions should consider cumulative exposure history, not just the presenting injury, and total career injury load should be a factor in ongoing RTP and load management frameworks.

citation: Khalil LS, Jildeh TR, Abbas MJ, McIntosh MJ, Sokoli A, Cominos N, Okoroha KR. Career Longevity and Performance Following Shoulder Instability in National Football League Athletes. Arthroscopy: The Journal of Arthroscopic and Related Surgery. 2021. doi: 10.1016/j.arthro.2020.12.225.
abstract: This retrospective case-control study investigated career longevity, game utilization, and performance of NFL athletes following glenohumeral instability events treated operatively versus nonoperatively from 2000 to 2019. Ninety-seven NFL players sustained a first-time shoulder instability event; 91 (93.8%) returned to play. Final analysis included 58 operatively managed and 33 nonoperatively managed players. Operatively managed players played significantly more seasons following RTP (4.1±2.7 vs. 2.8±2.5; P=0.015) and experienced less recurrent instability (27% vs. 50%; P=0.035) with longer intervals before recurrence (105.7±100.1 vs. 24.7±40.6 weeks; P<0.001). No significant differences existed in games played, games started, snap counts, or performance (SAV) between cohorts.
tldr: NFL players who undergo surgical stabilization following shoulder instability return to play with similar workload and performance as nonoperatively managed players but experience greater career longevity, fewer recurrent instability events, and longer time between instability events. Nonoperative management offers faster initial RTP but is associated with higher recurrence risk and shorter careers.
methods: Retrospective case-control study. NFL players with first shoulder instability events from September 2000 to February 2019 identified via public internet sources (team websites, injury reports, sports news). Inclusion required prior NFL experience, RTP after injury, and no prior ipsilateral upper extremity surgery. Players categorized as operative or nonoperative based on final treatment at end of index season. Outcomes: seasons played post-RTP, games played/started, offensive/defensive snap count percentage (OSCP/DSCP), season approximate value (SAV), recurrent instability rate, and interval to recurrence. SAV collected from Pro Football Reference. Statistical analysis: t-tests, Wilcoxon rank sum, chi-square, Fisher's exact, repeated measures ANOVA; significance p<0.05.
findings:
• 97 NFL players with first shoulder instability identified; 91 (93.8%) returned to play.
• Quarterbacks significantly more likely to undergo immediate surgery vs. other positions (P=0.023).
• Operative group played significantly more seasons post-RTP (4.1±2.7 vs. 2.8±2.5; P=0.015).
• Time to RTP significantly longer for operative group (36.62±10.32 vs. 5.43±12.33 weeks; P<0.05).
• Recurrent instability significantly lower in operative group (27% vs. 50%; P=0.035).
• Interval to recurrent instability significantly longer in operative group (105.7±100.1 vs. 24.7±40.6 weeks; P<0.001).
• No significant differences in games played, games started, OSCP, DSCP, or SAV between operative and nonoperative cohorts.
• Performance metrics (SAV) did not significantly change before vs. after injury for either cohort.
limitations: Retrospective design with reliance on publicly available data may introduce reporting inaccuracies. Players who did not return to play were excluded, potentially introducing selection bias toward more successful outcomes. Treatment group classification relied on news reports and absence of in-game data rather than surgical records. Limited ability to account for rehabilitation quality, team medical staff variation, or player motivation. Level of evidence: III.
practicalImplications: For NFL athletes with glenohumeral instability, surgical stabilization provides meaningful career longevity benefits and significantly reduces recurrent instability without compromising performance upon return. Medical staff should counsel players that operative management involves a longer initial recovery (~9 months vs. ~1 week nonoperative) but substantially reduces the likelihood of reinjury (27% vs. 50%) and supports a longer career. Quarterbacks appear particularly likely to elect surgery, likely reflecting the high performance demands of the position.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to NFL football. Shoulder instability prevalence in NFL players is reported at 10–14%. Defensive players represent the largest proportion requiring surgical management. Position-specific shoulder stability demands should inform strength and conditioning programming, particularly for defensive players and quarterbacks. Pre-participation screening for shoulder laxity and prior instability may help identify players at elevated risk requiring prophylactic programming.
rtp: Directly relevant to RTP decision-making. Nonoperative management allows faster initial RTP (~5 weeks) but carries 50% recurrent instability risk and shorter careers (2.8 vs. 4.1 post-injury seasons). Surgical stabilization requires ~37-week RTP timeline but reduces recurrence to 27% and extends career longevity. Staff should use shared decision-making weighing the team's season timeline against the player's career investment. Performance outcomes (snap count %, SAV) are equivalent regardless of management choice, supporting that full return to prior performance level is achievable via both pathways.

citation: Muench JR, Sinson GP, Glaeser B, Shah AS, Rowson S, Duma S, Mihalik J, Brooks A, Cameron K, Giza C, Goldman J, McGinty G, Riggen LD, Pasquina PF, Broglio SP, McAllister T, McCrea MA, Harezlak J, Stemper BD. Change in Head Impact Exposure Following Return-to-Sport in Concussed Football Athletes. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise. 2025. doi: 10.1249/MSS.0000000000003833.
abstract: This study examined how head impact exposure (HIE) changed during return-to-sport (RTS) following sport-related concussion in 52 concussed NCAA Division I college football athletes from six programs (2015–2019 seasons) using the helmet-based Head Impact Telemetry (HIT) System. Daily volume of head impacts after concussion was compared to pre-injury levels and to team- and position-matched non-concussed controls. Seventy-five percent of concussed athletes reduced HIE in their immediate RTS period compared to pre-injury levels, and over 40% never reached their pre-injury HIE at any point during the remainder of the concussion season. Concussed athletes had significantly lower HIE post-RTS compared to matched controls, and longer recovery duration was associated with larger reductions in HIE after RTS.
tldr: Most concussed college football athletes naturally reduce their head impact exposure upon returning to sport within the same season — 75% immediately reduced impacts and 40% never returned to pre-injury exposure levels — with longer recovery periods associated with larger post-RTS impact reductions. These findings suggest a natural conservative shift in playing behavior after concussion that may offer some neurological protection during the vulnerable post-injury window.
methods: Subset of NCAA-DoD CARE Consortium data from six NCAA Division I FBS programs (Air Force Academy, West Point, UCLA, UNC Chapel Hill, University of Wisconsin, Virginia Tech). Fifty-two concussed college football athletes with recorded contact days both before and after concussion within the same season were included; only first concussion analyzed. Each athlete equipped with helmet-based HIT System measuring head accelerations (>10 g threshold). Head impact data collected across all practice, scrimmage, and competition activities (2015–2019 seasons). Three time periods analyzed: Pre-Injury, First 7 Days Post-RTS, and >7 Days Post-RTS. Daily HIE compared to pre-injury baseline and to team- and position-matched non-concussed controls. Regression analysis used to examine association between recovery duration and change in HIE. Preseason and regular season segments analyzed separately.
findings:
• 75% of concussed athletes reduced HIE in their immediate RTS period compared to pre-injury levels.
• Over 40% of concussed athletes did not reach their pre-injury HIE level at any point during the remainder of the concussion season.
• Concussed athletes had significantly lower HIE post-RTS compared to team- and position-matched non-concussed controls over the same time period.
• Longer post-concussion recovery times were significantly associated with larger reductions in HIE after RTS.
• The reduction in HIE was most pronounced in the immediate days following RTS (First 7 Days Post-RTS window).
• Findings are consistent with the broader shift toward more conservative post-concussion management seen across multiple sports and playing levels.
limitations: Study limited to NCAA Division I FBS programs, reducing generalizability to non-elite or youth populations. Only athletes with recorded contact days both before and after concussion within the same season included, which may exclude more severely concussed athletes. HIE measured only via HIT System accelerometers (linear acceleration); rotational components not fully captured. Psychological factors (fear of reinjury, playing style changes) were not directly measured. Only first concussion per athlete included, limiting examination of repeat concussion effects.
practicalImplications: Concussed athletes naturally reduce head impact exposure upon RTS, which aligns with conservative post-concussion management guidelines and may contribute to neurological recovery. Medical staff should be aware that while most athletes self-reduce their impact exposure post-RTS, over 40% still do not return to pre-injury baseline — a finding that may warrant ongoing HIE monitoring even after clinical clearance. Longer recovery periods appear to reinforce more conservative playing behavior, suggesting that appropriate recovery time has biomechanical as well as clinical benefits.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American college and professional football. The finding that athletes naturally reduce head impact exposure after RTS suggests a behavioral adaptation that may reduce re-concussion risk during the vulnerable post-injury window. Athlete development staff and sports scientists should track HIE in the immediate post-RTS period, particularly in the first 7 days, to identify athletes who are not reducing impact exposure as expected and may need additional guidance or load modification.
rtp: Directly relevant to RTP protocols. The study provides objective biomechanical data showing that most concussed football athletes reduce HIE upon RTS within the same season, supporting the neurobiological rationale for gradual RTS protocols. The association between longer recovery and greater HIE reduction post-RTS suggests that premature RTP may not only increase re-injury risk clinically but also biomechanically (less HIE reduction). Monitoring head impact exposure in the first 7 days post-RTS may serve as a useful objective metric to complement standard neurocognitive and symptom-based RTP criteria.
